# Transcrição: 06-Movement

- **Fonte:** 06-Movement
- **Duração:** 12:09
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Before we dive into movement, I want to talk to you about this concept in psychology they call anchors. Now, if you're familiar with it, great. If you're not, let me explain. Let's say for example, you know, sometimes on Facebook when it shows us Facebook memories and then you click on it and it shows you photos from 10 years ago. And then when you see those photos, what happens? A flood of emotions overwhelm you. You go, oh, do you remember that time when we first got our little puppy? Oh, do you remember that time when our son was only six months old? Oh, do you remember that time we ate that beautiful pad thai from that Thai restaurant? We take way too many photos of food. But then all of a sudden the flood, the flood of emotions, you know, all of a sudden you overwhelmed by these emotions. That's called a visual anchor. All of those emotions are anchored to that visual picture. Now another example of this is have you ever walked past, you know, you're in the city and then you walk past someone and you go, and then you smell the cologne that you're one of your ex-girlfriend used to wear. And then all of a sudden you get flooded back of the memories of how she used to not let you hang out with the guys, not allowed to go to the gaming nights and then you have to stay home, watch the movies with her that you didn't particularly like, but you have to pretend you liked because you really liked her at the time. And then she invited you out to see her girlfriends, but I usually don't let you see your guyfriends and then you have to be in conversations that you don't want to be in and then eat food that you don't want to eat and then... Look, that's not a real story. Obviously not. Obviously not a real story. Or is it? Or is it a traumatic story of mine? No, it is. It's actually a truth. It's... And that's an example of a smell anchor. When you smell something, it brings back PTSD from past relationships. Now, the next one is an example of, you know, when you're driving down an old street or your old neighborhood and then all these memories come back of your childhood when you were growing up. Oh, that's the street I used to walk down with my best friend as we were going to school. That's an example of a spatial anchor. So, so far I've given you examples of visual anchors, smell anchors, spatial anchors. There's even such thing as auditory anchors. When you hear a song and then it just moves you back into the past. For example, for me, when I hear Backstreet Boys as long as you love me, takes me straight back to when I was 13, 14 years old. So these are examples of anchors. And when we're talking about movement, the anchor that's the most relevant is spatial anchors. And there are four ways we can use the space those around us when we're presenting and when we're communicating. The first one is to show time. And you've got to remember that you are opposite the audience. So when you're using the space around you to display time, you have to remember that the future is on your left and the past is on your right. The future is on your left and the past is on your right.

## [03:04 - 06:06]

And you've got to remember this because you have the tendency to go, no, no, no, but the future should be on your right and the past should be on your left. And you're going to feel like it should be that way because it is that way for you. But you have to remember you're opposite the audience. So you have to flip what feels right for you. And I'll give you an example of what I mean. If I said to you, even in this video, I said, Hey, let's go back to the past about three years ago. Doesn't that feel a little bit strange to you watching the video right now? Because for you, the past should be that side. So compare that versus if I said, Hey, let's go to the past about three years ago. And I walked this way. That seems more natural, doesn't it? I used to have to put a P up on my right side on stage right here. And I used to have to put a P there and a F over there just to remind me that's the past and that's the future. And you can use that now with that knowledge. You can use the space here to show time because you can say, you know, if you would go three years ago, I was still in the US and I was living in Southern California, but if you fast forward three years from then, now I'm in Australia. But when I look forward into the future, I feel like I do want to try a stint in Asia and I want to live in Asia for a period of my life. All of a sudden I was able to use the space that I have here to show time. Maybe this is three years into the future. This is the present and this is three years into the past. That's how I can use movement to show time. Next, I can use the space around me. I can use movement to show sequence. And this is when you're chunking the information down to help people process the information better. For example, if I was teaching communication, I would say that, look, the first thing we're going to talk about today in the session is we're going to talk about vocal foundations. And then the second thing we're going to talk about is, is how to build more awareness. I'm going to teach you a process called record and review. And then the third thing we're going to talk about is we're going to talk about storytelling. But let's begin with vocal foundations. Let's learn how to use our instrument well. Notice what I did there. I use number one, the first thing we're going to talk about, vocal foundations. Second thing I want to talk about, do you remember what it was? Self-awareness, the record and review process. And then the third thing we're going to talk about was storytelling. So I use the spaces right here to be able to chunk down what we're going to talk about. So you're anchoring to this position, vocal foundations. And then you're anchoring to this position over here, storytelling. And what that allows you to do is as you're presenting, you can go back to any of those positions to remind people what we're going to talk about. So say you've spoken about all three things, but then you say, but remember, the most important thing I'd say is storytelling, because people don't do this. People go, Oh, storytelling is not effective. It's just, you can't use it in a professional setting. I can use this space to remind them about all the things we spoke about during storytelling. So this is a wonderful way to anchor different topics to different points on

## [06:06 - 09:06]

the stage or whatever platform you're using. Now, do keep in mind, just like with time, that the past is here and the future is there. When you're doing sequencing, you've got to remember that the numbers go down this way and up that way, meaning one is always going to be here. Then two is here, then three is here. Whereas for a lot of people, when they don't know this, they do it the other way around because it feels natural for them to do it the other way around. Because if I said the first thing's here, the second thing's here. And then the third thing's here, it feels a little bit strange, doesn't it? These are the little one percenters. When you get all of these things right, people often say, Oh, this person seems like a natural on stage. What are they talking about? They're talking about getting these little things right. When you see someone, they seem a bit unnatural. It's because every time they speak and they say, Oh, the first thing we're going to talk about is this, second thing is this, and then third thing is this. You know, your conscious brain doesn't really notice it, but your unconscious brain goes, something feels a little bit off here. So it's very useful to learn this and get these things right. And you will come across very natural. To come across natural takes work. It's kind of goes against logic, but it's just the truth. It requires practice. Now, the third thing that you can do with movement is you can use it to show growth. So the whole situation of, Hey, we started here, now we're here. That kind of thing. So you can use it to show growth. That in 2001, when we started the business, we were here, but now moving forward to where we are now, we're in a completely different place financially. So you can use it to show growth. Same rule applies. Smaller amounts of growth here, but as you grow, you move up this way. So that's how you can use it for growth as well. You can also use movement to show location changes. You know, I often tell in the story that, in my keynote, you'll hear me say that, Oh, my parents went to the kitchen. And then when I say my parents went to the kitchen, this space over here was the kitchen. And then this space over here was the lounge room. And I just kind of playing it out for them by showing a change in location. I could also say I used to live in Australia, but now I live in America or I used to live in America, but now I live in Australia. This just allows you to move. And when you move, it shows that you have more presence. And presence is when you're using more of the space that you've been given. You're using up more of the space that you've been given. Often think about when you attended a conference last and you saw someone use a lectern and they stood behind the lectern for the whole 30 minutes or 45 minutes or I'm sorry if it's an hour because damn, that would have been tough. But they stood behind the lectern for like 45 minutes, let's say, and they spoke from that lectern and didn't move away from that lectern. You're often thinking in the back of your head, oh, gosh, this person is lacking a lot of presence. And we think this in the back of our heads, even sometimes unconsciously, because they've been given this entire, often massive stage. They didn't use any of it.

## [09:06 - 12:09]

They just kind of stood there the entire time. That's what I'm talking about when I'm talking about presence. It's you using your body and using the space in front of you. Now, it's not just about using it mindlessly. The biggest rule you hear about in movement is this. Move with purpose. Move with purpose. What you find is when people are just moving around aimlessly and there's no reason to their movement, what happens? You know, if I just move like this the entire time and I just kind of kept going back and forth like this, what happens to you? This gradually will start to distract you from the message, doesn't it? So you only move when there's a reason for movement, when you want to show time, when you want to show a change in location, when you want to be able to show growth, that's when you use movement. And now that you're learning about this, you know when to use movement. Otherwise, if there's no reason to move, don't move. Let me show you what happens when people don't move with purpose. Let me let me throw you to a video and I'm just going to grab the video from here. OK, I'm going to take this video and I'm just going to throw it at you. It was that inspiration that I got, that growth mindset that was sparked by these women designers that that embraced me and taught me so much to go and inspired me to go back to school. Without that, I wouldn't be where I am today. And the other thing I wanted to mention was that you see what happened there. What happens was when people don't move with purpose, it is distracting and you get distracted. Now, there's one more way to use movement that's purposeful. And the final way that you can use movement is to bring the characters to life and you remember me doing this in the storytelling section where in the story of how I met my wife, I was playing my wife and then I was playing myself. So these are all the reasons that you can use movement. These are all the all the all the different ways that you can use movement to bring the stories to life, to bring yourself to life, to give you more presence and to always and then to also move your voice. Because remember, if you move your body, it moves your voice. Don't be the speaker that stands behind the lectern. Be the speakers that moves away from that because the lectern hides your body as well. It's bad for communication because usually half your body is gone. So I want you to make this commitment with me. Hashtag no more lecterns. No more lecterns. Don't use them anymore and don't use the excuse that. Oh, but but the but the microphones there. Get a lapel. Get a lapel and clip it on yourself. Production companies or where you're generally speaking, they always have lapels. Don't give me the excuse of I don't have a microphone. Use a use a lapel. All right, my friends, these are the different ways in which you can use movement. I hope you will start to use more movement in your presentations and when you communicate to others, even if it's a small team meeting, take the opportunity to stand up and use the space that's around you and use body language to support the things you're saying and actually to enhance the things that you're saying.
